contentItems.textFragment: osiah was eight years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem thirt

contentItems.title: Key Points: Selection from 2 Kings 22 | NIV Bible | YouVersion
contentItems.content:
*   **Early Righteousness:** Josiah's reign began at a very young age (8), yet he was committed to doing what was right in the eyes of the Lord. This highlights the potential for individuals to choose righteousness even from a young age.
*   **Following David's Example:** Josiah's commitment to following the ways of his father David indicates a desire to emulate a positive role model who was known for his heart after God. This emphasizes the importance of godly examples and the transmission of faith through generations.
*   **Unwavering Obedience:** The phrase "not turning aside to the right or to the left" suggests Josiah's unwavering commitment to God's commands. This speaks to the importance of wholehearted obedience and resisting the temptation to compromise one's faith.
*   **Divine Approval:** The affirmation that Josiah "did what was right in the eyes of the Lord" signifies God's approval of his actions. This underscores the importance of seeking God's will and living in accordance with His standards.

contentItems.title: 2 Kings 22 | NIV Bible | YouVersion
contentItems.content:
Get the app
2 Kings 22
NIV
Parallel
2 KINGS 22
The Book of the Law Found
1Josiah was eight years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem thirty-one years. His mother’s name was Jedidah daughter of Adaiah; she was from Bozkath. 2He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord and followed completely the ways of his father David, not turning aside to the right or to the left.
3In the eighteenth year of his reign, King Josiah sent the secretary, Shaphan son of Azaliah, the son of Meshullam, to the temple of the Lord. He said: 4“Go up to Hilkiah the high priest and have him get ready the money that has been brought into the temple of the Lord, which the doorkeepers have collected from the people. 5Have them entrust it to the men appointed to supervise the work on the temple. And have these men pay the workers who repair the temple of the Lord— 6the carpenters, the builders and the masons. Also have them purchase timber and dressed stone to repair the temple. 7But they need not account for the money entrusted to them, because they are honest in their dealings.”
8Hilkiah the high priest said to Shaphan the secretary, “I have found the Book of the Law in the temple of the Lord.” He gave it to Shaphan, who read it. 9Then Shaphan the secretary went to the king and reported to him: “Your officials have paid out the money that was in the temple of the Lord and have entrusted it to the workers and supervisors at the temple.” 10Then Shaphan the secretary informed the king, “Hilkiah the priest has given me a book.” And Shaphan read from it in the presence of the king.
11When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his robes. 12He gave these orders to Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam son of Shaphan, Akbor son of Micaiah, Shaphan the secretary and Asaiah the king’s attendant: 13“Go and inquire of the Lord for me and for the people and for all Judah about what is written in this book that has been found. Great is the Lord’s anger that burns against us because those who have gone before us have not obeyed the words of this book; they have not acted in accordance with all that is written there concerning us.”
14Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam, Akbor, Shaphan and Asaiah went to speak to the prophet Huldah, who was the wife of Shallum son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas, keeper of the wardrobe. She lived in Jerusalem, in the New Quarter.
15She said to them, “This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says: Tell the man who sent you to me, 16‘This is what the Lord says: I am going to bring disaster on this place and its people, according to everything written in the book the king of Judah has read. 17Because they have forsaken me and burned incense to other gods and aroused my anger by all the idols their hands have made, my anger will burn against this place and will not be quenched.’ 18Tell the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of the Lord, ‘This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says concerning the words you heard: 19Because your heart was responsive and you humbled yourself before the Lord when you heard what I have spoken against this place and its people—that they would become a curse and be laid waste—and because you tore your robes and wept in my presence, I also have heard you, declares the Lord. 20Therefore I will gather you to your ancestors, and you will be buried in peace. Your eyes will not see all the disaster I am going to bring on this place.’ ”
So they took her answer back to the king.

2 Kings 22

22
The Book of the Law Found
1Josiah was eight years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem thirty-one years. His mother’s name was Jedidah daughter of Adaiah; she was from Bozkath. 2He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord and followed completely the ways of his father David, not turning aside to the right or to the left.
3In the eighteenth year of his reign, King Josiah sent the secretary, Shaphan son of Azaliah, the son of Meshullam, to the temple of the Lord. He said: 4“Go up to Hilkiah the high priest and have him get ready the money that has been brought into the temple of the Lord, which the doorkeepers have collected from the people. 5Have them entrust it to the men appointed to supervise the work on the temple. And have these men pay the workers who repair the temple of the Lord— 6the carpenters, the builders and the masons. Also have them purchase timber and dressed stone to repair the temple. 7But they need not account for the money entrusted to them, because they are honest in their dealings.”
8Hilkiah the high priest said to Shaphan the secretary, “I have found the Book of the Law in the temple of the Lord.” He gave it to Shaphan, who read it. 9Then Shaphan the secretary went to the king and reported to him: “Your officials have paid out the money that was in the temple of the Lord and have entrusted it to the workers and supervisors at the temple.” 10Then Shaphan the secretary informed the king, “Hilkiah the priest has given me a book.” And Shaphan read from it in the presence of the king.
11When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his robes. 12He gave these orders to Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam son of Shaphan, Akbor son of Micaiah, Shaphan the secretary and Asaiah the king’s attendant: 13“Go and inquire of the Lord for me and for the people and for all Judah about what is written in this book that has been found. Great is the Lord’s anger that burns against us because those who have gone before us have not obeyed the words of this book; they have not acted in accordance with all that is written there concerning us.”
14Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam, Akbor, Shaphan and Asaiah went to speak to the prophet Huldah, who was the wife of Shallum son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas, keeper of the wardrobe. She lived in Jerusalem, in the New Quarter.
15She said to them, “This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says: Tell the man who sent you to me, 16‘This is what the Lord says: I am going to bring disaster on this place and its people, according to everything written in the book the king of Judah has read. 17Because they have forsaken me and burned incense to other gods and aroused my anger by all the idols their hands have made,#22:17 Or by everything they have done my anger will burn against this place and will not be quenched.’ 18Tell the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of the Lord, ‘This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says concerning the words you heard: 19Because your heart was responsive and you humbled yourself before the Lord when you heard what I have spoken against this place and its people—that they would become a curse#22:19 That is, their names would be used in cursing (see Jer. 29:22); or, others would see that they are cursed. and be laid waste—and because you tore your robes and wept in my presence, I also have heard you, declares the Lord. 20Therefore I will gather you to your ancestors, and you will be buried in peace. Your eyes will not see all the disaster I am going to bring on this place.’ ”
So they took her answer back to the king.

contentItems.title: Area capture from Bible Chronology of Kings of Judah, Israel Solved! divided kingdom 931 - 587 BC
Synthesis: Kings 22
contentItems.content:
Okay, here's a synthesized summary based on the provided content item:

**Summary of 2 Kings 22:1-2 Regarding Josiah's Reign:**

This passage introduces King Josiah, emphasizing his youth at accession (8 years old) and the length of his reign (31 years in Jerusalem). The central theme is Josiah's righteousness and obedience to God. He is praised for doing "what was right in the eyes of the Lord" and for emulating his ancestor David, demonstrating unwavering faithfulness ("not turning aside to the right or to the left").

**1. Common Themes:**

*   **Righteous Kingship:** The primary theme is the importance of a king ruling righteously and in accordance with God's will.
*   **Obedience:** Josiah's obedience is explicitly highlighted as a defining characteristic of his reign.
*   **Following David's Example:**  The passage establishes a benchmark of righteousness by referencing David, a revered figure in Israelite history.

**2. Scripture References:**

*   2 Kings 22:1-2

**3. Key Theological Insights:**

*   **Divine Approval of Righteous Leadership:** The passage suggests that God approves of and blesses leaders who follow his commands and act justly.
*   **Importance of Historical Precedent:** The reference to David underscores the importance of learning from and emulating righteous figures from the past.

**4. Progression or Development of Ideas:**

*   The passage serves as an introduction to Josiah's reign, setting the stage for later events by establishing his character as one of righteousness and obedience. This creates anticipation for how that righteousness will manifest itself in his actions as king.

This is a preliminary summary based on a single scripture selection.  As more items are added, the summary will be expanded and refined to incorporate additional themes, references, insights, and development of ideas.

contentItems.title: Selection from Acts 2 NIV - The Holy Spirit Comes at Pentecost - Bible Gateway
contentItems.content:
he Holy Spirit Comes at Pentecost
2 When the day of Pentecost came, they were all together in one place. 2 Suddenly a sound like the blowing of a violent wind came from heaven and filled the whole house where they were sitting. 3 They saw what seemed to be tongues of fire that separated and came to rest on each of them. 4 All of them were filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues[a] as the Spirit enabled them.
contentItems.textFragment: he Holy Spirit Comes at Pentecost 2 When the day of Pentecost came, they were al

contentItems.title: Key Points: Selection from Acts 2 NIV - The Holy Spirit Comes at Pentecost - Bible Gateway
contentItems.content:
The key theological insights and biblical truths from the provided passage (Acts 2:1-4) are:

*   **The Holy Spirit's Arrival:** Pentecost marks the official arrival and outpouring of the Holy Spirit upon the believers. This is a pivotal moment in redemptive history, fulfilling Old Testament prophecies and inaugurating the New Covenant era.
*   **Divine Initiative:** The event is initiated by God ("Suddenly a sound...came from heaven"). This emphasizes that the Holy Spirit is a gift from God, not something earned or manufactured by humans.
*   **Corporate Experience:** The Holy Spirit is poured out on the believers "all together in one place," highlighting the importance of Christian community and unity in receiving God's blessings.
*   **Empowerment for Witness:** The filling of the Holy Spirit is directly linked to the ability to "speak in other tongues as the Spirit enabled them." This suggests that the Spirit's primary purpose is to empower believers for missional outreach and cross-cultural communication of the Gospel.
*   **Supernatural Manifestation:** The Holy Spirit's arrival is accompanied by dramatic and supernatural signs (violent wind, tongues of fire). These manifestations demonstrate the power and reality of God's presence and action in the world.
*   **The Holy Spirit is for All Believers:** The text states that "All of them were filled with the Holy Spirit," implying that the gift of the Spirit is available to all who believe in Jesus Christ, regardless of background or status.

contentItems.content:
New International Version (NIV)
Bible Book List
Font Size
Passage
Resources
Hebrew/Greek
Your Content
Acts 3
New International Version
Peter Heals a Lame Beggar

3 One day Peter and John were going up to the temple at the time of prayer—at three in the afternoon. 2 Now a man who was lame from birth was being carried to the temple gate called Beautiful, where he was put every day to beg from those going into the temple courts. 3 When he saw Peter and John about to enter, he asked them for money. 4 Peter looked straight at him, as did John. Then Peter said, “Look at us!” 5 So the man gave them his attention, expecting to get something from them.

6 Then Peter said, “Silver or gold I do not have, but what I do have I give you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk.” 7 Taking him by the right hand, he helped him up, and instantly the man’s feet and ankles became strong. 8 He jumped to his feet and began to walk. Then he went with them into the temple courts, walking and jumping, and praising God. 9 When all the people saw him walking and praising God, 10 they recognized him as the same man who used to sit begging at the temple gate called Beautiful, and they were filled with wonder and amazement at what had happened to him.

Peter Speaks to the Onlookers

11 While the man held on to Peter and John, all the people were astonished and came running to them in the place called Solomon’s Colonnade. 12 When Peter saw this, he said to them: “Fellow Israelites, why does this surprise you? Why do you stare at us as if by our own power or godliness we had made this man walk? 13 The God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, the God of our fathers, has glorified his servant Jesus. You handed him over to be killed, and you disowned him before Pilate, though he had decided to let him go. 14 You disowned the Holy and Righteous One and asked that a murderer be released to you. 15 You killed the author of life, but God raised him from the dead. We are witnesses of this. 16 By faith in the name of Jesus, this man whom you see and know was made strong. It is Jesus’ name and the faith that comes through him that has completely healed him, as you can all see.

17 “Now, fellow Israelites, I know that you acted in ignorance, as did your leaders. 18 But this is how God fulfilled what he had foretold through all the prophets, saying that his Messiah would suffer. 19 Repent, then, and turn to God, so that your sins may be wiped out, that times of refreshing may come from the Lord, 20 and that he may send the Messiah, who has been appointed for you—even Jesus. 21 Heaven must receive him until the time comes for God to restore everything, as he promised long ago through his holy prophets. 22 For Moses said, ‘The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your own people; you must listen to everything he tells you. 23 Anyone who does not listen to him will be completely cut off from their people.’[a]

24 “Indeed, beginning with Samuel, all the prophets who have spoken have foretold these days. 25 And you are heirs of the prophets and of the covenant God made with your fathers. He said to Abraham, ‘Through your offspring all peoples on earth will be blessed.’[b] 26 When God raised up his servant, he sent him first to you to bless you by turning each of you from your wicked ways.”

Footnotes
Acts 3:23 Deut. 18:15,18,19
Acts 3:25 Gen. 22:18; 26:4
Acts 2
Acts 4

contentItems.title: Area capture from Bible Insights Maps - POC

analysis.description:
Here's an analysis of the image:

1.  **Brief Description:** The image shows a Bible study tool interface. On the left, there's a "Bible Reader" section displaying various Bible verses (KJV) and their locations. On the right, there's a map centered on Gaza, with a marker indicating its location. Below the map, there's a section for study notes.

2.  **Text/Diagrams/Charts:**

    *   **Bible Verses:** Genesis 12:6, Genesis 12:8, Joshua 10:1, Judges 16:1, 2 Samuel 2:1, Acts 9:3, Jonah 1:3.  These verses mention place names like Sichem, Bethel, Jerusalem, Gaza, Hebron, Damascus, Tarshish, and Joppa.
    *   **Map:** A geographical map displaying the city of Gaza.
    *   **Map Coordinates:** Gaza's coordinates are listed as 31.5, 34.466667.
    *   **Map Attribution:**  "Leaflet | Tiles © Esri - Esri, DeLorme, NAVTEQ, TomTom, Intermap, IPC, USGS, FAO, NPS, NRCAN, GeoBase, Kadaster NL, Ordnance Survey, Esri Japan, METI, Esri China (Hong Kong), and the GIS User Community" indicates the map's data source.
    *   **Other Text:** "Bible Insights Explorer," "Genesis," "Search scripture...", "Bible Reader (KJV)," "Study Notes (Auto-saves)," "Type your notes here...", "For His Glory," "Auto-saved at 11:57:01 PM," "Test Bench (Jerusalem)."

3.  **Key Observations Relevant to Bible Study or Sermon Notes:**

    *   **Geographical Context:** The tool emphasizes the importance of geographical context in understanding the Bible. By linking verses to a map, it allows users to visualize where events took place.
    *   **Place Names:** The verses highlight the significance of specific locations in biblical narratives. For example:
        *   **Gaza:**  The mention of Gaza in Judges 16:1 relates to the story of Samson and his encounter with a harlot. This location can be further explored in the context of Samson's overall narrative and the Philistine territory.
        *   **Bethel:**  Genesis 12:8 mentions Bethel in relation to Abraham's travels. Bethel later becomes a significant place of worship.
        *   **Jerusalem:** Joshua 10:1 mentions Jerusalem. This is an important city in the Bible, especially in the Old Testament.
        *   **Hebron:** 2 Samuel 2:1 mentions Hebron where David inquired of the Lord.
        *   **Damascus:** Acts 9:3 mentions Damascus where Paul had a light from heaven.
        *   **Joppa:** Jonah 1:3 mentions Joppa where Jonah went down to.
    *   **Travel and Movement:**  Many of the verses describe travel and movement between locations. This highlights the dynamic nature of the biblical narratives and the importance of understanding the routes and distances involved.
    *   **Study Notes:** The "Study Notes" section encourages users to record their observations and insights, promoting deeper engagement with the text.
    *   **"Test Bench (Jerusalem)":** This could indicate a specific feature or study focus related to Jerusalem, perhaps allowing users to explore the city's history and significance in more detail.
    *   **"For His Glory":** This is a common phrase used to express the purpose and motivation behind studying the Bible, emphasizing a desire to honor God through understanding His word.

contentItems.title: Key Points: Selection from Bible Insights Maps - POC
contentItems.content:
Okay, here are the key theological insights and biblical truths that can be extracted from Genesis 12:8, focusing on its potential significance beyond a simple geographical description:

*   **Seeking Higher Ground for Worship:** The verse mentions Abraham moving to a *mountain* on the east of Bethel. Mountains in the Bible are often associated with encountering God, prayer, and sacrifice. Abraham's choice of a mountain suggests a desire to be closer to God, perhaps seeking a more intimate and elevated spiritual experience.

*   **Strategic Positioning for Witness:** Abraham strategically positions himself with Bethel ("House of God") to the west. This suggests that he deliberately chose a location where he could witness to the people of Bethel, bringing the knowledge of God to them.

*   **Tent as a Symbol of Pilgrimage and Dependence:** Abraham *pitched his tent*. The tent represents his temporary dwelling, emphasizing his status as a sojourner and pilgrim. It highlights his dependence on God for provision and protection during his journey, trusting that God will lead him to the promised land.

*   **Balance of Contemplation and Action:** The verse implies a balance between seeking personal communion with God (on the mountain) and engaging with the surrounding community (Bethel). It shows that faith involves both inward spiritual growth and outward witness and service.

*   **Claiming the Land Through Worship:** By building an altar and calling on the name of the Lord, Abraham is effectively claiming the land for God. He is establishing a spiritual foothold in the territory, declaring God's sovereignty over it.

These points highlight the spiritual significance of Abraham's actions and location, revealing his commitment to God, his desire to witness to others, and his dependence on God's provision.

verses.matchedText: He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord and followed completely the ways of his father David
All the believers were together and had everything in common.
They sold property and possessions to give to anyone who had need.
Every day they continued to meet together in the temple courts. They broke bread in their homes
praising God and enjoying the favor of all the people. And the Lord added to their number daily
All the believers were together and had everything in common.
They sold property and possessions to give to anyone who had need.
‘The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your own people
‘Anyone who does not listen to him will be completely cut off from their people.’
‘Through your offspring all peoples on earth will be blessed.’
Genesis 12:8 - And he removed from thence unto a mountain on the east of Bethel, and pitched his ten
